name: Abu Hudhaifa ibn Utbah
epithet: The One Who Sought Another Status
title: Abu Hudhaifa ibn Utbah (RA): The One Who Sought Another Status
meta_description: The story of Abu Hudhaifa ibn Utbah (RA), the son of a chief of Quraysh who walked away from a throne he was born to inherit, made three migrations, and longed only for a place with Allah.
keywords: Abu Hudhaifa ibn Utbah, story of Abu Hudhaifa, son of Utbah ibn Rabia, Salim mawla Abi Hudhaifa, people of Badr, battle of Yamama, sahaba stories
lede: Some men are born far from power and spend their lives reaching for it. This man was born at the very top, with a clear path to becoming the most senior figure of Quraysh, and he quietly walked away from all of it.

This is the story of Abu Hudhaifa ibn Utbah (may Allah be pleased with him): a son of one of Makkah's leading houses who chose persecution and exile over a crown, and who asked Allah for one thing only, a status that no city could give him.

paras: Abu Hudhaifa is one of the companions who is easy to overlook. We are not even certain of his given name. It was some form of Hashim, and that is as close as the record takes us. He is usually remembered through others: through his father, or through Salim, the freed servant he raised as a son.

Even his title carries a question. Abu Hudhaifa means the father of Hudhaifa, yet he had no son by that name. Some say a child named Hudhaifa died young and was never spoken of again. Others say he took the name from a nephew, in the years before Allah blessed him with children of his own. The name simply stayed with him.

What the sources do agree on is his character. He was tall, dignified, and known for manners that people did not forget. He was gentle, humble, and generous. He spoke very little. He was counted among the best of the children of his clan, and that reputation went before him long before Islam reached his heart.

His mother, Fatima bint Safwan, was divorced from his father and accepted Islam early. She made the migration to Abyssinia and passed away there, never reaching Madinah, never seeing the religion she believed in take its final shape. Faith ran through this family in scattered lines, and Abu Hudhaifa would carry it further than most of them.

To understand what he gave up, you have to understand who surrounded him. He was from Banu Abd Shams, one of the most influential tribes of Makkah, and he was the son of its chief. His connections reached into the other leading houses of the city.

His father was Utbah ibn Rabia, regarded as the eldest of the leaders of Quraysh. His sister was Hind, the wife of Abu Sufyan. His father-in-law was Suhayl ibn Amr, another of the great negotiators of Quraysh. His foster brother, through nursing, was Musab ibn Umayr. He was, by birth and by ties, woven into the very centre of Makkah's elite.

Had he simply waited, time alone would likely have made him the most senior figure of his people. The leadership he was poised to inherit was the same leadership his father held. He did not have to reach for it. He only had to stay where he was.
That is the weight of what he set down. Not a distant hope of power, but a near and certain one, the kind other men spent their lives and their feuds chasing. He saw it clearly, and he let it go.

His father, Utbah, was not the most vicious of the Prophet's opponents. While others beat the drums of war, he wanted to find a compromise. He believed the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ might have a price, and that if Quraysh paid it, their world could stay intact.

So Utbah went and sat beside the Prophet ﷺ and made his offer. If it was wealth he wanted, they would gather their riches and make him the richest among them. If it was status, they would make him their leader, and no decision would be taken without him. If it was kingship, they would make him their king. If he was unwell, they would find a cure and spend lavishly until he was healed.
The Prophet ﷺ listened to the very end. Then he asked, gently, whether Utbah had finished. When Utbah said yes, the Prophet ﷺ did not answer a single one of the offers. He began, instead, to recite:

ayah.text: A revelation from the Lord of Mercy, the Giver of Mercy; a Scripture whose verses are made distinct as a Quran in Arabic for people who understand, giving good news and warning. Yet most of them turn away and so do not hear.

sections.h2: A face that came back changed
paras: Utbah sat and listened to the recitation as it continued, verse after verse, until the Prophet ﷺ reached a verse of prostration and bowed down in sajdah. When he rose, he told Utbah simply: you have heard what you have heard, and now it is for you to decide.

Utbah walked back to the leaders of Quraysh, and they saw at once that something had moved in him. They said he had returned with a different face from the one he left with. He told them plainly: this is not poetry, this is not sorcery, this is something divine. Leave this man alone.

They refused, and Utbah, a man of his tribe, fell back into line and kept up the opposition. The Quran had changed his face, but he would not let it change his heart. His son made the opposite choice. Abu Hudhaifa let it change everything.

Abu Hudhaifa accepted Islam early, together with his wife Sahla bint Suhayl and the young Salim. They were among the first. His own father did not beat him, for Utbah loved him and shielded him. But the pressure of those circles became too heavy, and he left for Abyssinia with his family.

He became one of the rare companions who made three migrations: twice to Abyssinia, and then to Madinah. His son Muhammad was born in exile. He fought beside the Prophet ﷺ in every battle, including Badr, and he kept his place there the way he had always lived: quietly, in the background, a soldier who did not need to be seen.

Think about what this meant. His peers, the sons of the other chiefs, would not accept Islam until many years later. Abu Hudhaifa was the first of that class to break ranks, the first to stand on the opposing side from the men he had grown up beside, though he, more than any of them, had been poised to lead.

There is a saying of the Prophet ﷺ that fits him exactly: people are like precious metals, and the best of them in the days of ignorance are the best of them in Islam, when they understand. The good character Abu Hudhaifa had carried all his life did not vanish when he believed. It carried straight over, into a swift acceptance of Islam and a quiet, faithful place beside the Prophet ﷺ.

At Badr, the battle opened with a duel, as was the custom. Three men stepped forward from the side of Quraysh. They were his father Utbah, his uncle Shayba, and his brother. He watched his own family walk out to fight, and when they were killed, he wept.

If you had seen him crying that day, you might have thought he regretted his path. He did not. He told the Prophet ﷺ that he had no doubt at all in his decision, none. His grief was for something else. He only wished his father had accepted Islam before that day, because he had seen the signs in him, and he had loved him.

It is one of the few personal scenes the record preserves of him. A man who had given up everything for this religion, standing on the battlefield, not regretting the cost, but quietly wishing the people he loved could have walked the road with him.

sections.h2: A father to Salim, and a final wish
paras: Abu Hudhaifa is most often remembered through Salim, the freed servant he had taken as his own. He treated Salim exactly as he treated his biological children, so that Salim never felt different from the rest. Later he would marry Salim to one of his nieces. He was a man willing to set tradition aside for the sake of someone he loved and the justice he owed him.

When the practice of claiming an adopted child as one's own blood was set aside in Islam, it touched this household closely. Salim had grown up in their home as a son. Sahla brought the difficulty to the Prophet ﷺ, and a concession was given so that Salim could remain in the house as before. He stayed simply Salim, the freed servant of Abu Hudhaifa, because his parents were unknown, and the bond between them never weakened.

Abu Hudhaifa lived to see the conquest of Makkah, and he hoped to the end that those left of his family would believe. One brother, Abu Hashim, did: he became a companion, then a scholar of the religion who settled in Syria and was still teaching in the time of Uthman. Even his sister Hind, who had once ordered the mutilation of the Prophet's uncle Hamza, repented and accepted Islam in the end.

What Abu Hudhaifa wanted for himself, though, was simpler. He longed to be a martyr, to have that place with Allah, and he carried that hope through every battle. It was granted at Yamama, in the hard fight against the false prophet Musaylimah, where over five hundred companions were martyred in a single day. Fighting beside him that day was Wahshi, the man who had killed Hamza years before, now turning his spear against Musaylimah. His two sons left no children, and so no lineage of his survives today. What survives is something else: the memory of a man who was offered the world through his father and turned toward Allah instead, and who, in the end, was given exactly the status he had asked for.

lessons_heading: What Abu Hudhaifa's life teaches us
lessons_intro: His story is not loud. It is the quiet record of a man who kept choosing the lasting thing over the immediate one, again and again, until it cost him his life and earned him what he wanted most.

lessons.bold: Seek a status that does not fade.
lessons.text: He was offered wealth, rank, and a throne through his father. He wanted only a place with Allah, and measured his life by that alone.

lessons.bold: Conviction can hold without certainty about others.
lessons.text: He wept for his family at Badr yet never doubted his own path. Loving people and following the truth are not the same thing, and he managed both.
He was known as the best of his clan before Islam, and that gentleness, humility, and generosity simply continued into his faith.

lessons.bold: Serve without needing to be seen.
lessons.text: He could have been the most senior figure of Quraysh. He chose instead to be a quiet soldier in the background, content with his place.

faq.q: Who was Abu Hudhaifa ibn Utbah?
faq.a: He was an early companion of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ and the son of Utbah ibn Rabia, one of the leading chiefs of Quraysh. He accepted Islam early, made three migrations, fought at Badr, and was martyred at the battle of Yamama.

faq.q: Why is he called Abu Hudhaifa if he had no son named Hudhaifa?
faq.a: The sources are unsure. One view is that a son named Hudhaifa died young. Another is that he took the title from a nephew in the years before he had children of his own. The name stayed with him.

faq.q: What was his connection to Salim?
faq.a: Salim was a freed servant whom Abu Hudhaifa raised as his own son, treating him no differently from his biological children. Abu Hudhaifa's story is most often told alongside Salim's.

faq.q: What can we learn from the life of Abu Hudhaifa?
faq.a: To seek a lasting status with Allah over worldly power, to hold firm to the truth even while grieving those who do not share it, and to serve quietly without needing recognition.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: Most of us will never be offered a throne. But all of us are offered smaller versions of Utbah's bargain every day: a little more standing, a little more comfort, a quieter conscience, if only we will look away from what we know is right. Abu Hudhaifa shows what it looks like to refuse, calmly, and to keep refusing.

He asked Allah for one thing and arranged his whole life around it. That is the quality worth taking from him into an ordinary day. Not the battles, not the lineage, but the steady habit of asking what actually lasts, and then choosing it, even when the people you love are standing on the other side.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Abu Hudhaifa ibn Utbah (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). The Quran translation is from The Qur'an, M.A.S. Abdel Haleem (Quran 41:2-4). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

His father. His uncle. His brother.

He raised a freed servant, Salim, as his own son.

**He was offered the world and chose a status no city could give.**

Seek the thing that lasts.

May Allah be pleased with him.
social.caption:
He was born into one of Makkah's leading houses, the son of a chief, with a clear path to becoming the most senior figure of Quraysh. Abu Hudhaifa ibn Utbah (may Allah be pleased with him) walked away from all of it.

When his own father was sent to offer the Prophet ﷺ wealth, leadership, even a crown, the answer was only the Quran. Utbah came back shaken but would not change. His son changed everything: he accepted Islam early, made three migrations, and stood on the opposing side from the men he grew up with. He asked Allah for one thing, a place with Him, and was martyred at Yamama having received it.

Which part of his story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

email.subject: He was born to rule Makkah, and walked away
email.alt_subject: The son of a chief who chose another status
email.preview: He was offered wealth, leadership, even a crown through his father. He wanted only a place with Allah.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

Some people are born far from power and spend their lives reaching for it. Abu Hudhaifa ibn Utbah (may Allah be pleased with him) was born at the very top, and quietly walked away.

His father, Utbah ibn Rabia, was one of the leading chiefs of Quraysh. His sister was the wife of Abu Sufyan. Had he simply waited, time alone would likely have made him the most senior figure of his people. He did not have to reach for power. He only had to stay where he was.

There is a famous scene with his father. Utbah was sent to offer the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ a deal: wealth, leadership, even a crown, a cure if he was unwell, anything, if he would only give up his message. The Prophet ﷺ listened to the whole offer. Then he answered none of it, and instead began to recite the Quran.

Utbah came back to Quraysh with a changed face. "This is not poetry," he told them. "This is not sorcery. Leave this man alone." They refused, and Utbah fell back into line. The Quran had moved his face, but he would not let it move his heart.

His son made the opposite choice. Abu Hudhaifa accepted Islam early, made three migrations, and became the first of the chiefs' sons to break ranks with his own class.

At the battle of Badr, three men stepped out to fight from the enemy side: his father, his uncle, and his brother. He wept when they were killed. But he told the Prophet ﷺ that he had no doubt at all in his path. He only wished his father had accepted Islam first.

He asked Allah for one thing: to be a martyr, to have that place with Him. At the battle of Yamama, he was granted exactly that.

There is a quiet lesson in him. Most of us will never be offered a throne, but all of us are offered smaller versions of that bargain: a little more comfort, a little more standing, if we will only look away from what we know is right.

May Allah be pleased with him, and grant us the wisdom to seek the status that lasts.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: Some men are born far from power and spend their whole lives reaching for it. Abu Hudhaifa ibn Utbah (may Allah be pleased with him) was born at the very top. He was the son of Utbah ibn Rabia, one of the leading chiefs of Makkah, and a clear path lay before him to become the most senior figure of his people. He only had to wait.

He was known, even as a young man, for gentleness and good manners. He was tall and dignified, generous, and so quiet that people remembered his silence. He was counted among the best of his clan long before Islam reached him.

His father once went to the Prophet ﷺ with an offer from all of Quraysh: take whatever you want, wealth, leadership, a crown, but give up this message. The Prophet ﷺ listened to every word, then answered none of it. He simply recited the Quran. Utbah came back with a changed face and told his people to leave this man alone. But he would not let the words change his heart.

His son chose the opposite road. Abu Hudhaifa accepted Islam early, and when the pressure in Makkah grew too heavy, he left for Abyssinia. He became one of the few who migrated three times. He was the first of the chiefs' sons to break ranks and stand on the opposing side from the men he had grown up beside.

At the battle of Badr, three men came out to duel from the enemy lines: his father, his uncle, and his brother. He watched them fall, and he wept. Yet when the Prophet ﷺ came to comfort him, he said he had no doubt at all in his choice. He only wished his father had accepted Islam before that day, because he had loved him.

He raised a freed servant named Salim as his own son, and treated him no differently from his own children. And for himself, he asked Allah for one thing only: to be a martyr, to have that place with his Lord.

He was granted it at the battle of Yamama, in the hard fight against a false prophet, where many companions were lost in a single day. He left no surviving lineage. What he left instead was the example of a man who was offered the whole world and turned, instead, toward Allah.
storybook.pullquote: You have heard what you have heard, and now it is for you to decide.
storybook.reflection: Abu Hudhaifa was offered, through his father, everything most people spend their lives chasing: wealth, rank, a throne. He wanted only a status with Allah, and arranged his whole life around it. Most of us will never refuse a crown, but we are offered smaller bargains every day. His life asks a single quiet question: when you choose, do you choose what lasts?
storybook.source: His story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
